speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Henry Frankel. So you were telling us about when you surrendered. What bunker were you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Kupiecka Street and Meiselsa Street, 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose bunker was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a bunker for one of the people working with collaboration with Mordechai Anielewicz. Actually, these people did not have anything to do with the bunker. But we knew about this bunker because the people were, prior to this, working for some other German industry, and they were stranded. But they had the bunker. And they were willing to survive together with the other fighters of the Warsaw ghetto. Now, this was on May the 5th, 1945. When we got out of the bunker, we-- and the Germans experienced that two dogs died from the teargas that they threw in. We-- they became absolutely furious. They made us walk in into the bunker and bring up these dogs by hand. And of course one of us could not carry it. So two of us had to carry the dog out. And there was absolutely no life in this dog. And when we brought him out, the Germans were beating us up in-- without mercy. And with hands up, we did in a running thread, we had to-- we got to Zamehofa Street, where some other people from other bunkers were selected and brought to Umschlagplatz. The distance was about 15 minutes of walking to Umschlagplatz. So it was not the severe conditions of a-- or undertaking something-- a long march. When--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any bystanders?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There were no bystanders. The houses were already devastated by the artillery of the Germans who were pounding at the ghetto without any mercy, to destroy it completely. There were fires all over the place, and smoke. And the-- the ruins of the Warsaw ghetto were just beyond any comprehension. People could not understand or visualize what actually look like, the same quarters that we were enjoying so much at the beginning of the outbreak of the war, and the present time, when we were leaving the ghetto. But at least with our heart, we knew very well that we'd done this, what a human being's supposed to do-- to fight for his life with all the power of mankind, to show the Germans that it's not a easy task just to bring people and gas them and cremate them. We knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the soldiers treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Without mercy. They had bayonets and they were poking at us. And also, many of them had whips. They were in what they called a running thread brought us to the Umschlagplatz. Whipping us and hitting us constantly. We are not allowed to take anything, water, even-- not even the smallest carrying case or whatever we could have a hold in our hands. We had to run and deliver-- get to the Umschlagplatz. Once we got to the Umschlagplatz, condition were beyond any human understanding. The releases of the people on the staircases, in the corners, the smell of deterioration and the smell of death. That you could smell death. Overloaded quarters of former school that the-- from which the Germans were shipping people to camps of Treblinka, Poniatowa, Trawniki, Majdanek, and many others. There was no trust. There was total disorganization. People did not ask for food. They will-- people did not ask for anything else but for some water. And water was cut off. There was no water coming in. And you can imagine the-- the-- what condition were in the toilets and the cleaning quarters without any water, and how people had to release them in the worst cases, on the staircases and corners and so on. It was very depressing condition. And you really didn't care anymore whether this is the end of the life or just escape to-- to something else. But we knew that sooner or later, it would not last very long. With the-- under condition like this, no human being can exist. And finally, after two days, with some calm restored and some promises made and some provision given us, with a quarter of a-- a round bread, they packed us up in railroad trains and-- without mercy, packing us up to a point of just standing one to-- one-- one to another. Because there was no space to sit down or-- or-- there again, there-- there were no sanitary provisions made by Germans. And packed up and-- I think that cattle would have been treated with a better condition than the human being was treated at that time. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with any other groups of Jews then? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- the entire transport of some 10 people-- 10 wagons of people packed up to-- to a maximum. I think that in a wagon like this there were between 100, 125 people. And there were just standing room only. And everybody was grasping for air coming through the two openings on both sides of the wagon. And the door were closed, sealed. And the train finally, sometime in the afternoon, left. And on the way, we were passing such places as Siedlce. And when I have seen the sign, 'Siedlce,' the-- it's a little town between Warsaw and Treblinka-- I knew that we are on our way to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about Treblinka then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that there is a total-- this total annihilation place of people. There's-- nobody ever came out out of it, or any information came out of it. The only thing we knew, that Treblinka was in existence and was a working crematorium place blasting full-- going full blast. When we arrived to Treblinka, we-- was quite late at night and we are standing in--

speaker: interviewer
text: What day was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the 7th of May, 1943. And it was quite late at night. They let us stay in this wagon until early morning hours, and around 7 o'clock or 8 o'clock, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew. We knew. We have seen-- we are passing by the sign, 'Treblinka.' There was a small sign on a-- the railroad tracks to Treblinka. We knew that we are there, that we came to Treblinka. I remember that as they were loading every one of the lorrie-- the wagons, there were screams and beating and shouting and shooting and-- and without any mercy. You could tell that people were beaten to-- without any mercy. And when we finally open up our wagon, that they came out and they said, we are looking for young, healthy people, young, healthy people. They keep repeating, young-- the-- half of the wagon were young, healthy peoples that were standing. And then SS--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was repeating this?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was repeating this? Who was saying this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were German SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: In German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were Germans who were-- Germans with some Ukrainians. In typical Ukrainians' uniforms with SS insignia on the-- on their collars. And we knew right away where we were without any hesitation.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know they were Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: They spoke-- because they spoke Russian. Russian, which is similar to Ukrainians. And when they were talking to the Germans, there was a typical accent of Slavic-- Slavic accent in German. And we knew right away that this is-- in what hands we have found ourself. And the Germans kept just pulling out, one by one, somebody, and forming a column of young men. And I was found myself among them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were carried away. They were practically whipped away from us, especially women and elderly, and poked with bayonets, with the guns with-- guns with the bayonets mounted. And even shooting and kill right on the platform. This was the selection platforms, quite famous.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw people get shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But you were so absorbed with your own self that you actually didn't pay attention. The shooting and killing of people were-- of-- did not make any effect on us anymore. There was too much of a-- of a-- worrying about-- about the next step of yourself. And not that we didn't worry about the others, but we knew exactly what is going on. The screams-- you can't describe this by any means to any human being, to what extent a human cry and-- and-- and scream can carry, especially people who were suddenly torn apart-- the men from women, and men-- women from men, and the younger children, or people who were sick, or not feeling-- or couldn't walk, or had any deficiency in walking and things like this were immediately liquidated by a-- an SS man, or he was Ukrainian, come from behind, putting a-- a gun to their head and shooting them on the spot. Then I-- we heard the screams of some of the SS men-- don't waste any bullets on Jews. Don't waste any bullets on Jews. Let them die. And we knew that the-- the day is coming where-- the hour is very-- coming very close to-- to the day of departure. And somehow we're standing like, glued on to one another in this column. And I can't tell how many were there. There were-- for the two last wagons of the-- of the transport. And I would say there were between-- or maybe three-- there were somewhere around 300 people, youngsters, fairly healthy-looking and in good shape, that the-- the Germans suddenly send in some people from the camp. And we knew that they were from the-- from Treblinka, because they had the striped uniform and the numbers on 'em. And they were cleaning out whatever people left behind. And the minute they were done cleaning, taking everything out, the Germans marched us-- us right in into this same wagon. And it was quite surprising to us. And one of the inmates from Treblinka said, in Jewish, you are saved. You are saved. Don't worry. You are saved. And we couldn't understand what he meant. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he look to you? What-- How did he look, his appearance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Healthy, fairly healthy-looking. Because they were getting additional rations for the people from the Sonderkommando. And they were getting always something, or stealing, or organizing. They never said in a camp that somebody stole something. They said that they were organizing it. And this the way things were-- actually functioned. But [INAUDIBLE] when we-- we when they-- they packed us up in the last two wagons, then we-- they closed up the doors and they gave us water to drink. And this was quite unusual. Germans never would give anybody anything, especially in a transport to-- to any other place. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you been outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: For about two hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the camp? Could you look at the [? compound? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: From far away, we saw the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from the platform, when you stay in Treblinka, even today, from the selection platform, you see thousands of gravestones overlooking the-- the main camp. And there were barracks visible quite-- from-- from a distance. Quite a large camp. And we didn't know that-- how big it was or what people were doing, if there were people there. Because it was known to us that the minute you arrive to Treblinka, you go directly to gas chambers and crematoria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the crematoria, or could you smell?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but we smelled the crematoria. There was a typical-- typical case of smell that couldn't be describe. Burning flesh. This is a-- a-- nausea-causing condition that you cannot absorb. You try to forget it. You try to escape it. And you-- you can't avoid it. It was right there in-- with the slightest wind, you received the-- the smell of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The same that you'd smelled at Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, similar as Majdanek. Right. With Majdanek, the story was that the-- if the winds were blowing the-- the smell of crematoria from us, then it was half-bearable. But if it was blowing towards us, we constantly were praying that perhaps the end should come as soon as possible, without special suffering. But when we got to these trains, the last three wagons were packed up and we were brought to Majdanek. It was at--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Before we-- before we go there, how did the camp-- Treblinka-- look, compared to Majdanek? Did you notice any similarities or differences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah--

speaker: interviewer
text: From what you could see?

speaker: interviewee
text: --there was a great difference. Majdanek didn't have no railroad landing. Treblinka had a railroad landing. And the selection was conducted right on the landing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they conduct the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very simply. They were-- the transport was cordoned off by the Ukrainians holding guns at ready. And Germans came and make the selection of right and left, and right and left, right and left. In many cases, at the later stages, when we came from Warsaw, there was not even a selection made, except the group of healthy young men to Majdanek. There-- there was not a selection made. They were just pushing and shoving these people and killing them with-- hitting them and whipping them without mercy. And the screams-- if one think about-- hears screams of a crying mother losing a child, well, you can imagine how thousand of them sound.

speaker: interviewer
text: There were still children at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They-- I don't remember any children being with the people from the-- on this transport. But there were very young people, like 12 or 14. And they were of very fragile structure. Because they were growing up under very severe conditions. There was not a sufficient amount of food for them to develop in the early-- in the early stages of their life, for be prepared for better life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any dogs around? Or-- any dogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. All-- all around. All around were dogs and people who were constantly watching over from the towers. There were also towers around this place. There-- where the German were observing any movement or anything unusual coming to the-- going to-- towards the crematoria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many towers that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three towers placed on the outside of the incoming train and two on the other side. Then there was-- on the right-hand side, as we entered Treblinka, to the left was the selecting platform. And on the right-hand side there were the three towers. On the selecting place, there were two towers. And when we left, that was some time after noon. They kept us in the wagons and we arrived sometime in the evening--

speaker: interviewer
text: At Majdanek. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: At Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: At Treblinka, did you see any non-soldiers, any civilians, or anybody out of uniform? Did you see anybody who wasn't in uniform at Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Have either uniform or-- or there were people who-- the inmates carrying-- and when we asked them, they were saying, all Jews. There were no Gentiles or other ethnic groups involved in-- in Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear or see anything else that you remember, there, at Treblinka?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not of great evidence. But when we got to Treblinka, there was a-- a unusual desire to pray. And where we were getting out of the wagon, the people were saying, [HEBREW]. And we somehow had a feeling that perhaps these are the last moments of our life and why not to ask for forgiveness at the last moments. And evidently, the almighty was listening to us. Because we somehow survived Treblinka and were shipped to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any women with your shipment? Or-- any women or just young men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just young men. When we arrived to Majdanek, they put us on a-- in a-- into a warehouse. We slept on the floor without anything. In the morning we got-- were woken up by 5 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You recognized it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lublin. We came to Lublin. To the railroad siding of the railroad station. And so they unloaded us to the warehouse of-- one of the warehouses around the railroad station. The distance from Lublin to Majdanek was not very far. And even the slower group could make it in half an hour, walking. And-- I mean marching. And for the good morning, we receive a good beating of the SS men waiting for us with the whips and just whipping us without any mercy. Then they ask us to stand still. The beating stopped. And we received small pieces of bread with some so-called coffee. That was the imitation of coffee made by Germans and-- but this was like a blessing to us, after not eating for more than three days. And they form in a group and we march through Majdanek. When we got to Majdanek, the-- I have seen that the third field was-- from the road, you could see the camp. This was a road towards Zamosc, southeastern part of Poland. And we somehow managed to see the camp. And I have seen that the third field, the fourth field, and the fifth field were finished and ready to absorb all the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was just praying that if anything happens, that it should happen very quickly. Because the days of martyrdom don't [INAUDIBLE] are not accepted with great-- great endeavor. Did you recognize the camp? Had it changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had it changed much?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They added more watchtowers. Right from the road. And there were signs that anybody trespassing-- in German-- anybody trespassing would be shot on the spot. And the Polaks didn't take no chances there. Because beside us, on the upside of that road from Lublin, there were administration houses where the commander of the camp was living and high-ranking officers. We got to the-- finally to the camp and I was-- one-- the one worry was on my mind, whether they will recognize me. And I had longer hair. And then as bad as I did look without shaving and things like this, I decided to prevail under the same name that I enter first time. I didn't change my name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when they asked me, what's your name? I said Henry Frankel. And when were you born? And they wrote-- there was a SS man writing it very quickly in. And then he-- all he gave me is the-- a red triangle and a yellow triangle, two of them, for the-- for the trousers and for the tunic, and gave me the number of 14285.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we got to the-- to the washrooms, which were used also for gassing, I look at the washroom and I said to the man, this the end of our line. This is not a washroom. This is gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were used before as a experimental thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the camp earlier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And at that time, the people were calling me idiot. There's no such thing existing. However, the people from the Sonderkommando who were collecting our clothing were talking-- you know what it is, the Sonderkommando what I mentioned. These are people who were selected from a-- a group of prisoners to conduct certain services during these selections or through the washing or new transport oncoming. Collecting their clothes, collecting their shoes, and so on. And some of them were talking in Yiddish. And they were saying, you don't have to worry today about the gas chamber because they don't have no gas. And they were saying to us in Yiddish. And we look at one another and say, let's hope that they know what they are talking about. But when we enter there, these chambers, they told us, when they will be giving you the soap, then you will be convinced. Then the German came out and start distributing the soap among the people as they were entering the chambers. And he said, don't worry about your clothing. You will get it clean, without any lice, on the other side of the chambers. And we somehow were-- were nodding, and thanking him, and getting into the chambers. And then we embraced one another, saying, if anything happen, let's at least say goodbye to one another. And just fortunately, that the water came out. It was cold and simple. But it was so sweet that there is no description of any of such experience in life. Just like a soldier that was coming back from a battle front and wanted to wash up, after so many weeks of not being able to take a shower, this shower was like sent from heaven. And once we are done--
